# `*.chassis.ron` — field reference

<!-- GENERATED by `cargo run -p dc_data_docs` (dronecom #2815). Do not edit: field docs live as /// comments on the Rust structs. -->

Data: [`definitions/chassis/`](../definitions/chassis/)

## `ChassisDefinition`

Top-level chassis definition loaded from a `.chassis.ron` asset file.

Each file defines a discrete named chassis with fixed stats.

### `id` : [`ChassisId`](#chassisid)

Stable slug identifying this chassis (e.g. `"mq_220"`). Referenced by
blueprints and save files; distinct from the display `designator`.

### `designator` : [`Designator`](#designator)

Military-style hull designation shown in the UI (e.g. `"MQ-220"`).
Combined with `codename` by `Self::full_name` into the full name.

### `codename` : [`Codename`](#codename)

Codename shown in the UI (e.g. `"Lynx"`) — the second half of the full
display name, e.g. "MQ-220 Lynx".

### `domain` : [`Domain`](#domain)

Physical environment this chassis operates in (Air / Sea / Submarine).
Drives navigation, the default NTDS class, and propulsion-signature
validation.

### `launch_requires` : `Vec`<[`Facility`](#facility)>

Facilities required on the carrier to launch this entity.
Must contain at least one entry.

### `recovery_requires` : `Vec`<[`Facility`](#facility)>

Facilities required on the carrier to recover this entity.
Empty means expendable (no recovery).

### `provides` : `Vec`<[`FacilityProvision`](#facilityprovision)>

Facilities this chassis provides when mounted as a carrier component,
each with its parallel capacity (cell/tube/rail count).

### `length` : f32

Physical length in metres, explicit per chassis.

### `weight` : f32

Empty (bare-hull) structural weight in kg — excludes components, carried
payload, and fuel. The payload budget is `max_weight - weight`.

### `max_weight` : f32

Maximum loaded weight in kg (MTOW) — the ceiling for structure +
components + carried payload + fuel. Exceeding it fails blueprint
validation.

### `max_fuel` : f32

Fuel-tank capacity in kg (full tank). The blueprint loads
`max_fuel * fuel_tank_percent`; total loaded weight (structure +
components + carried + fuel) must stay within `max_weight` (MTOW).

### `hit_points` : f32

Structural health. Warhead damage subtracts from it; the vehicle is
destroyed when it reaches zero.

### `drag` : f32

Lumped horizontal drag area — drag coefficient × reference area (m²).
Higher drag means a lower top speed: it sets the air-domain thrust = drag
equilibrium speed (see `terminal_max_speed`). Sea and submarine top
speed is thrust-to-weight and ignores this value; rotary-wing vertical
motion uses the separate, larger `vertical_drag_area`.

### `mast_height` : f32

Sensor-mast height in metres above the hull. Raises mast-mounted sensor
detectors/emitters (see `crate::MountingPosition`) for over-the-horizon
geometry. Distinct from a submarine's `sail_height`.

### `em_signatures` : `Option`<[`EmSignatures`](#emsignatures)>

Electromagnetic signature (radar / IR / visual detectability). `None` for
a platform with no EM signature — undetectable by those sensors.

### `acoustic_signatures` : `Option`<[`AcousticSignatures`](#acousticsignatures)>

Acoustic signature (passive engine/propeller noise + active sonar
reflectivity). `None` for a platform with no acoustic signature —
undetectable by sonar.

### `physics` : [`PhysicsParams`](#physicsparams)

Domain-specific movement parameters (altitude/depth band, turn, draft);
the `PhysicsParams` variant selects the movement model.

### `propulsion` : [`PropulsionSignature`](#propulsionsignature)

Propulsion signature (DC-36.6) — the feature a signature-capable sensor
reads to classify platform type independent of speed.

### `engine` : [`ChassisEngine`](#chassisengine)

Built-in engine (thrust/fuel). Distinct from `propulsion`, which is the
sensor-classification signature. `Unpowered` for mines / gravity bombs.

### `allowed_components` : `Vec`<[`ComponentFilter`](#componentfilter)>

Component-compatibility whitelist: a component may be mounted only if it
matches at least one filter. Empty means any component is allowed.

### `carrier` : bool

Whether this chassis can serve as a team's command carrier — the
mothership/flagship that hosts and commands a fleet. `false` for drones,
munitions, and escorts.

### `ntds_classification` : [`NtdsClassification`](#ntdsclassification)

How the NTDS symbol class is determined for this chassis.

### `target_domains` : `Vec`<[`TargetDomain`](#targetdomain)>

Domains this weapon chassis can target. Empty for non-weapon chassis.

### `guidance_uplink` : [`GuidanceUplink`](#guidanceuplink)

Midcourse guidance-uplink capability (DC-946.2). `FromLauncher` for
guided munitions that read their launcher's fire-control refinement in
midcourse; `None` for autonomous platforms, drones, and unguided
ordnance. Surfaced as a `dc_vehicle::GuidanceUplink` component at
launch.

### `battery_capacity_kwh` : f32

Battery capacity in kWh — powers electronics after fuel depletion.

### `base_power_draw_kw` : f32

Base power draw in kW — flight computer, datalink, avionics.

### `engine_bells` : [`EngineBells`](#enginebells)

Per-chassis engine-order bell throttles (#1430). Each slow bell
(`loiter`/`cruise`/`full`) is offered iff its throttle is authored;
`Flank` is always available (max throttle) and `Stop` is physics-gated on
`min_speed == 0`. A fire-and-forget munition authors all-`None` (only
`Flank`). The designed-cruise anchor for endurance/range estimates is
`EngineBells::cruise_reference` (see `dc_types::FuelCurve`).

## `ChassisId`

Identifier for a chassis definition, used as a registry lookup key.

### `0` : `String`

The chassis definition's registry key string.

## `Designator`

Military-style designator (e.g. "MQ-220", "TF-340").

### `0` : `Arc`<str>

The designator string (e.g. "MQ-220").

## `Codename`

Human-readable codename (e.g. "Lynx", "Gale").

### `0` : `Arc`<str>

The codename string (e.g. "Lynx").

## `Domain`

Operational domain for a chassis or entity.

Variants:

- **`Air`** — Operates in the air — a fixed- or rotary-wing aircraft.
- **`Sea`** — Operates on the sea surface — a ship or surface craft.
- **`Submarine`** — Operates below the sea surface — a submarine.

## `Facility`

Launch/recovery facility type on a carrier.

Variants:

- **`FlightDeck`** — Flight deck (runway / catapult) — launches and recovers fixed-wing aircraft.
- **`HeliPad`** — Helicopter pad — launches and recovers rotary-wing aircraft.
- **`WellDeck`** — Flooded well deck — launches and recovers surface craft (USVs) and submarines.
- **`AirLaunch`** — Air-launch rail carried by an aircraft — lets a parent aircraft carry and release munitions in flight.
- **`VLS`** — Vertical launch system — cells that launch missiles.
- **`TorpedoTube`** — Torpedo tube — launches torpedoes.

## `FacilityProvision`

A launch/recovery facility provided by a platform, paired with its
parallel capacity.

`parallelism` is the facility's count of independent cells/tubes/rails —
VLS cell count, torpedo tube count, air-launch rail count. It caps how many
weapons a `Parallel` deck-pipeline phase can hold concurrently.
FlightDeck/HeliPad/WellDeck pipelines are serial conveyors and ignore the
value (set it to `1`).

### `facility` : [`Facility`](#facility)

The `Facility` type this platform provides.

### `parallelism` : u8

Number of independent cells/tubes/rails of this facility (see the struct
docs for how it caps a `Parallel` deck-pipeline phase).

## `EmSignatures`

Electromagnetic signature profile — detectable by radar, IR, and visual sensors.

### `rcs` : f32

Radar cross-section (m²) — the signal strength a radar sensor sees.
Smaller is stealthier (e.g. ~0.03 for a low-observable drone); the sensor
attenuates it by range through the radar falloff law to decide detection.

### `ir_base` : f32

Infrared signature strength — the source level an IR sensor sees. Larger
is hotter and detectable further away.

### `visual_cross_section` : f32

Visual (optical) cross-section — the signal strength a visual sensor sees.
Larger is more detectable.

## `AcousticSignatures`

Acoustic signature profile — detectable by sonar sensors.

### `passive_noise` : [`PassiveSonarNoise`](#passivesonarnoise)

Engine/propeller noise (passive sonar) — speed-dependent.

### `sonar_cross_section` : f32

Acoustic reflectivity (active sonar) — a physical size, speed-independent.

## `PhysicsParams`

Domain-specific physics parameters with concrete values.

Variants:

- **`FixedWing`** — Fixed-wing airframe (drones, cruise missiles) — lift-borne flight with a stall-speed floor and a structural manoeuvre-G limit, holding an operating altitude band. Flown by the fixed-wing autopilot / flight model.
  - `min_altitude` : f32 — Lowest altitude (metres above sea level) the airframe holds in normal cruise — the floor of its operating band.
  - `max_altitude` : f32 — Highest altitude (metres above sea level) the airframe can reach — the ceiling of its operating band and its routing ceiling.
  - `default_altitude` : f32 — Altitude (metres above sea level) held when no altitude is commanded (its cruise altitude). Surfaced as the vehicle's default `AltitudeCommand`.
  - `min_attack_altitude` : `Option`<f32> — Minimum altitude when executing an Attack order (e.g. missile diving to sea level).
  - `max_attack_altitude` : `Option`<f32> — Maximum altitude when executing an Attack order.
  - `launch_speed` : `Option`<f32> — Initial launch speed override. When present, launch impulse uses this instead of computed `min_speed`. Allows missiles to define a booster velocity separate from cruise speed.
  - `max_pitch_rate` : f32 — Maximum pitch rate in rad/s — airframe structural limit.
  - `max_yaw_rate` : f32 — Maximum yaw rate in rad/s — airframe structural limit.
  - `stall_speed` : f32 — Minimum sustained flight speed in m/s — below this, lift < weight.
  - `max_lateral_g` : f32 — Structural maneuver-G limit (multiples of g) — the binding cap on commanded lateral acceleration across the useful speed band. IR dogfight missiles pull ~50-60 G; cruise missiles and large drones far less. No `#[serde(default)]` — every `FixedWing` chassis RON must declare it (#2044).
  - `supported_trajectories` : `Vec`<[`TrajectoryProfile`](#trajectoryprofile)> — Trajectory profiles this airframe can fly. Validated non-empty and always containing `TrajectoryProfile::Direct` (see `validate_chassis`). No `#[serde(default)]` — every `FixedWing` chassis RON must declare it.
  - `terrain_clearance` : f32 — Terrain-follow terrain-clearance height in metres — the altitude the vehicle holds above terrain when flying the `TerrainFollow` trajectory profile. Per-chassis (DC-258); replaces the global `SimConfig.air_terrain_clearance` for the terrain-follow hold target only. Pathfinding and reactive terrain avoidance still use the global value.
- **`RotaryWing`** — Rotary-wing airframe (helicopters) — powered-lift flight that can hover and holds an operating altitude band. Flown by the rotary-wing flight model, whose vertical climb/descent is limited by `vertical_drag_area`.
  - `min_altitude` : f32 — Lowest altitude (metres above sea level) held in normal flight — the floor of the operating band.
  - `max_altitude` : f32 — Highest altitude (metres above sea level) reachable — the ceiling of the operating band (a thrust-limited rotor may hover-ceiling below this under load).
  - `default_altitude` : f32 — Altitude (metres above sea level) held when none is commanded (cruise altitude). Surfaced as the vehicle's default `AltitudeCommand`.
  - `max_pitch_rate` : f32 — Maximum pitch rate in rad/s — airframe structural limit.
  - `max_yaw_rate` : f32 — Maximum yaw rate in rad/s — airframe structural limit.
  - `vertical_drag_area` : f32 — Lumped **vertical** drag parameter: Cd × reference area (m²) for motion along the rotor axis. Far larger than the chassis's (horizontal) `drag`, because a rotorcraft climbing/descending fights its own rotor disc and downwash, not its fuselage cross-section. This is what limits climb/descent to a realistic rate: a single horizontal `drag_area` would let the helicopter climb at its (much higher) forward-drag-limited terminal (#2087). No `#[serde(default)]` — every `RotaryWing` chassis RON must declare it.
- **`SeaSurface`** — Surface vessel (ships, USVs) — moves in two dimensions on the water plane (altitude fixed at sea level). Integrated by the surface-ship model.
  - `draft` : f32 — Hull draft in metres — how far the keel sits below the waterline.
  - `min_turn_rate_fraction` : f32 — Fraction (0.0–1.0) of the ship's maximum turn rate still available at zero speed. A hull needs way over its rudder to turn: it turns at the full rate near top speed and at only this fraction of it at rest (read by the surface-ship model's turn-rate-vs-speed curve).
- **`Submarine`** — Submarine — moves in three dimensions below the surface (negative world Y). Integrated by the submarine model.
  - `max_depth` : f32 — Maximum (crush) depth in metres, given as a positive number; the deepest the boat may operate. Its operating band is `[-max_depth, 0]`.
  - `default_depth` : f32 — Depth in metres (positive) held when no depth is commanded. Surfaced as the vehicle's default `AltitudeCommand` at `-default_depth`.
  - `draft` : f32 — Waterline → keel (metres): how deep the hull sits below the reference.
  - `sail_height` : f32 — Waterline → top of sail/hull (metres): the above-reference extent that must stay submerged. Drives the no-broach ceiling `-(sail_height + submarine_surface_clearance)` (#1983). Distinct from the sensor-mast `mast_height` on `ChassisDefinition`.
  - `min_turn_rate_fraction` : f32 — Fraction (0.0–1.0) of the boat's maximum turn rate still available at zero speed — the same way-over-the-rudder curve as a surface hull (read by the submarine model's turn-rate-vs-speed curve).
  - `min_attack_depth` : `Option`<f32> — Minimum depth (as positive number) when executing an Attack order.
  - `max_attack_depth` : `Option`<f32> — Maximum depth (as positive number) when executing an Attack order (e.g. torpedo diving deep).

## `PropulsionSignature`

A platform's propulsion signature — the feature a capable sensor reads to
classify type independent of instantaneous speed.

Variants:

- **`Rotary`** — Rotor-blade flash (helicopters) — radar micro-Doppler.
- **`Turbojet`** — Jet-engine modulation, JEM (fast fixed-wing) — radar.
- **`Propeller`** — Propeller modulation (slow fixed-wing UAVs) — radar.
- **`Rocket`** — Rocket motor (missiles) — no air-breathing modulation.
- **`Screw`** — Marine propeller blade-rate (ships, subs, torpedoes) — sonar DEMON.
- **`Pumpjet`** — Pump-jet — quiet, broadband (modern subs/torpedoes) — sonar DEMON.

## `ChassisEngine`

A chassis is either powered by a built-in engine or unpowered (gravity
bombs, mines, drifting objects — `VehiclePropulsion::Unpowered` at runtime).

Variants:

- **`Unpowered`** — No engine — gravity bombs, mines, and drifting objects. Contributes zero thrust and burns no fuel (`VehiclePropulsion::Unpowered` at runtime).
- **`Powered`**([`EngineSpec`](#enginespec)) — Driven by a built-in engine with the given `EngineSpec` (thrust, fuel burn, and altitude thrust response/schedule).

## `ComponentFilter`

Filter that matches components by category.

Variants:

- **`Category`**([`ComponentCategory`](#componentcategory)) — Matches any component in this `ComponentCategory`.

## `NtdsClassification`

How the NTDS symbol class is determined for this chassis.

Variants:

- **`Explicit`**([`NtdsClass`](#ntdsclass)) — Use this specific NTDS class (e.g., Missile, Torpedo).
- **`DeriveFromDomain`** — Derive from domain and physics model (Air/Helicopter/Surface/Subsurface).

## `TargetDomain`

What domains a weapon can target. Distinct from `Domain` (where a vehicle operates).
An air-launched missile operates in `Domain::Air` but targets `TargetDomain::SeaSurface`.

Variants:

- **`Air`** — Airborne targets — aircraft, helicopters, and missiles.
- **`SeaSurface`** — Sea-surface targets — ships and surface craft.
- **`Submarine`** — Submerged targets — submarines and torpedoes.
- **`Land`** — Land targets — stationary targets ashore.

## `GuidanceUplink`

Whether a launched vehicle can receive a midcourse guidance uplink from
its launcher's fire-control track (DC-946.2). `FromLauncher` = the weapon
reads its launcher's refinement in midcourse; `None` = autonomous /
own-seeker-or-base only.

Variants:

- **`None`** — No midcourse uplink — the weapon flies autonomously on its own seeker or the launch solution only.
- **`FromLauncher`** — The weapon receives a midcourse guidance uplink from its launcher's fire-control track, refining its aim in flight.

## `EngineBells`

The throttle fraction a chassis runs at for each *slow* engine-order bell.
A bell is offered iff its throttle is `Some`. `Flank` is implicit and always
available (throttle 1.0 → `max_speed`); `Stop` is not represented here — it
is physics-gated on `min_speed == 0` (see `Self::available`).

Replaces the former single `cruise_throttle: f32`. No `#[serde(default)]` —
every chassis RON authors this explicitly; a fire-and-forget munition
authors all-`None` (leaving only `Flank`).

### `loiter` : `Option`<f32>

Throttle fraction (0.0–1.0 of `max_speed`) for the Loiter bell, or `None`
if the chassis does not offer it.

### `cruise` : `Option`<f32>

Throttle fraction (0.0–1.0 of `max_speed`) for the Cruise bell, or `None`
if the chassis does not offer it. This is also the fuel/endurance anchor.

### `full` : `Option`<f32>

Throttle fraction (0.0–1.0 of `max_speed`) for the Full bell, or `None` if
the chassis does not offer it.

## `PassiveSonarNoise`

Speed-dependent passive sonar source level (engine/propeller noise).

Two-slope-with-base model: a constant machinery floor (`base`, never silent),
a gentle `quiet_noise_per_mps` slope below the cavitation inception speed, and
a steep `cavitation_noise_per_mps` slope above it. See
`docs/plans/2026-06-16-dc387-speed-dependent-sonar-noise-design.md`.

### `base` : f32

Always-on machinery floor (strength at zero speed).

### `cavitation_speed` : f32

Cavitation inception speed in m/s — the knee.

### `quiet_noise_per_mps` : f32

Strength gained per m/s below the knee (gentle).

### `cavitation_noise_per_mps` : f32

Strength gained per m/s above the knee (steep, `>= quiet_noise_per_mps`).

## `TrajectoryProfile`

How a missile shapes its vertical path from launch to target.

Variants:

- **`Direct`** — Fly straight at the target (baseline behaviour).
- **`TerrainFollow`** — Follow the terrain: hold clearance above max(ground, sea level), sampled at the vehicle XZ — hugs ridges over land, skims over water. High drag, short range, late detection.
- **`Lofted`** — Powered climb → cruise high in thin air → glide on a gravity arc after burnout → terminal dive. The single long-range profile: cruise altitude scales with launch range (farther targets loft higher), so a long shot banks more altitude and trades it back for downrange in an unpowered best-glide once the motor runs dry. Subsumes the former Ballistic profile.

## `EngineSpec`

Performance characteristics of a chassis's built-in engine. Weight and cost
are NOT here — they are folded into the chassis's own `weight` / `max_weight`
/ `cost` fields (the engine is structurally part of the chassis).

### `thrust_n` : f32

Peak (boost) thrust in Newtons — the full-throttle thrust during the boost
phase. A `ThrustSchedule::BoostSustain` engine delivers a fraction of
this during sustain; a `BoostCoast` engine holds it until fuel runs out.

### `fuel_burn_rate` : f32

Boost-phase fuel burn at full throttle, fuel units per minute.

### `idle_burn_fraction` : f32

Fraction of `fuel_burn_rate` consumed at idle (0.0–1.0).

### `thrust_response` : [`ThrustResponse`](#thrustresponse)

Whether thrust is constant or scales with air density — drives altitude
thrust scaling.

### `thrust_schedule` : [`ThrustSchedule`](#thrustschedule)

How thrust varies over the burn: a single `BoostCoast` pulse (every
existing chassis) or a `BoostSustain` boost→sustain→coast profile (the
real-AMRAAM energy-management motor).

## `ComponentCategory`

Category of a mountable component.

Variants:

- **`Sensors`** — Detection equipment (radar, sonar, RWR, IR, visual sensors).
- **`Warheads`** — Weapon payloads (warheads); at most one per vehicle.
- **`Utility`** — Support equipment with no sensor or warhead payload.
- **`CarriedEntities`** — Carried sub-vehicles / munitions — blueprints stowed aboard for launch.

## `NtdsClass`

NTDS contact classification -- determines the base symbol shape.
Combined with affiliation (derived at render time) to select the
specific NTDS symbol variant.

`CommandShip` is distinct from `Surface` -- it renders with a cross
overlay (circle+cross for friendly, diamond+cross for enemy).
Regular surface contacts are plain circle/diamond.

`Unknown` is the honest "no class evidence" value for the flat field
(DC-1050): a contact whose graded classification is still at the tree root
(`NtdsNode::Unknown`) and whose altitude is unresolved carries `Unknown`
rather than a false `Surface`. It is a *flat-field* state only — never a
tree leaf (`NtdsNode::Leaf`), never authored on a chassis, and never the
class of an owned vehicle. Consumers branching on environment (air vs
surface engagement, weapon selection) must treat `Unknown` as
"unclassified": take the conservative path until evidence resolves it.

Variants:

- **`Unknown`** — No class evidence yet — see the type-level docs. Flat-field only.
- **`Air`** — Fixed-wing aircraft (helicopters and missiles are separate classes).
- **`Helicopter`** — Rotary-wing aircraft — a helicopter (hover-capable).
- **`Surface`** — A surface vessel (ship or boat) floating on the sea surface.
- **`Subsurface`** — A submarine — a contact below the sea surface.
- **`Land`** — A stationary target on land.
- **`CommandShip`** — A carrier or other high-value command ship. Renders with a cross overlay to distinguish it from a plain `Surface` contact.
- **`Missile`** — A missile in flight (an airborne weapon).
- **`Torpedo`** — A torpedo running (a subsurface weapon).

## `ThrustResponse`

How an engine's thrust responds to air density (altitude). Names the
behavior, not an engine archetype: `Constant` thrust is what rocket motors
and surface/submarine propulsion share; `AirDensity`-scaled thrust is the
turbofan/turbojet/turboprop falloff. The propulsion archetype itself is
modelled separately (`PropulsionSignature`).

Variants:

- **`Constant`** — Thrust unaffected by altitude (density-independent).
- **`AirDensity`** — Thrust scales with air density ρ/ρ₀ (falls off with altitude).

## `ThrustSchedule`

How an engine's thrust varies over the burn — the single source of truth for
the boost / sustain / coast distinction.

The phase clock is **fuel consumed** (`capacity − current`): no per-entity
elapsed-time state exists or is needed, because for a flat-burn motor (idle
burn == max burn, true of every missile) fuel consumed is an exact proxy for
burn time. Keying on consumed fuel keeps the live sim, the reach flyout, and
the closed-form fallback in lockstep — all three call `Self::resolve`, so
there is exactly one place that decides the per-instant thrust and burn.

The out-of-fuel **coast** transition (thrust → 0) is *not* encoded here: it is
the caller's `has_thrust()` / `is_out_of_fuel()` gate, shared by every
propulsion variant (and correctly handling the zero-capacity expendable
edge). `Self::resolve` is consulted only while the engine is still powered.

Variants:

- **`BoostCoast`** — Single full-thrust phase until fuel exhaustion, then coast. The `FuelCurve` owns the burn rate (throttle-modulated). This is every existing chassis — drones, ships, the M-100 IR missile, the M-500 cruise missile — and the behaviour-preserving default.
- **`BoostSustain`** — Full-thrust **boost**, then a reduced-thrust **sustain** phase, then coast (the real-AMRAAM energy-management profile). The schedule owns the sustain burn rate; a solid-propellant boost-sustain motor does not throttle, so the burn is flat (see `Self::debug_validate`).
  - `boost_fuel_kg` : f32 — Fuel (kg) burned during boost; the remaining tank funds the sustain phase. Must be `> 0` and `<` the tank capacity.
  - `sustain_thrust_fraction` : f32 — Sustain thrust as a fraction of peak (boost) thrust. In `(0, 1)`.
  - `sustain_burn_rate` : f32 — Sustain fuel burn rate (kg/min) — lower than the boost burn rate.
